agent_output: {"executive_summary":"This synchronous-chain architecture (Order→Payment→Stripe) creates a cascading failure corridor where any downstream outage propagates upstream as customer-facing errors. The fire-and-forget notification path silently drops emails with no recovery. The design needs circuit breakers, retries with backoff, an outbox pattern for notifications, and a saga/compensation mechanism for partial failures across the Order-Payment boundary.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Accepts customer orders, orchestrates payment charging, triggers notifications","boundaries":"Owns order lifecycle (created→paid→confirmed→failed). Must not leak payment or notification internals into its domain model."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Abstracts payment processing, calls Stripe, returns charge result","boundaries":"Owns payment attempts, idempotency keys, charge status. Isolates third-party gateway details behind its own API."},{"name":"Stripe Gateway (External)","responsibility":"Third-party payment processing","boundaries":"Uncontrolled external dependency. Latency, availability, and error semantics are dictated by Stripe."},{"name":"Notification Service","responsibility":"Sends confirmation emails to customers","boundaries":"Owns delivery channel logic (email templates, provider integration). Should own its own retry/delivery guarantees."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Order Service","to":"Payment Service","contract":"POST /payments {orderId, amount, currency, idempotencyKey} → {paymentId, status, failureReason?}","coupling_type":"synchronous temporal coupling — Order blocks on Payment response"},{"from":"Payment Service","to":"Stripe Gateway","contract":"Stripe Charges API (synchronous HTTPS)","coupling_type":"synchronous temporal coupling — Payment blocks on Stripe response"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Notification Service","contract":"POST /notifications {orderId, customerEmail, type} — fire-and-forget","coupling_type":"fire-and-forget (no delivery guarantee, no retry, no acknowledgment)"}],"data_flow":"Customer → Order Service → (sync) Payment Service → (sync) Stripe → response back to Payment → response back to Order → (async fire-and-forget) Notification Service → Email Provider","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Circuit Breaker","rationale":"Prevent cascading failures when Payment Service or Stripe is degraded. Order Service stops calling a failing Payment Service after threshold breaches, failing fast instead of hanging."},{"pattern":"Retry with Exponential Backoff + Jitter","rationale":"Transient failures (network blips, 503s) on the Payment→Stripe path are recoverable. Jitter prevents thundering herd on recovery."},{"pattern":"Idempotency Keys","rationale":"Retries on the payment path MUST be safe. Without idempotency keys, a retry after a timeout could double-charge the customer — the single worst outcome."},{"pattern":"Timeout Budgets","rationale":"Each synchronous call needs an explicit timeout. Order Service sets an outer timeout; Payment Service sets a tighter inner timeout for Stripe. Prevents thread/connection pool exhaustion."},{"pattern":"Transactional Outbox","rationale":"Replace fire-and-forget notification with an outbox table written in the same transaction as the order status update. A relay process reads the outbox and delivers to Notification Service with at-least-once guarantees."},{"pattern":"Bulkhead","rationale":"Isolate the thread/connection pool used for Payment calls from other Order Service operations so a slow Payment Service cannot starve unrelated endpoints."},{"pattern":"Compensation / Saga","rationale":"If Order is marked 'paid' but a subsequent step fails, or if payment succeeds but order persistence fails, a compensation action (refund) must be triggered."},{"pattern":"Dead Letter Queue","rationale":"Failed notifications (after retries) land in a DLQ for manual inspection and replay, ensuring no customer email is permanently lost."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Ordering","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: orderId, states: created→payment_pending→paid→confirmed→failed, invariant: an order can only be charged once per idempotency key)"]},{"name":"Payments","classification":"core","aggregates":["PaymentAttempt (root: paymentId, tracks idempotencyKey, charge status, gateway reference, supports safe retry)"]},{"name":"Notifications","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["NotificationRequest (root: notificationId, tracks delivery status, retry count, last attempt timestamp)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Payments","downstream":"Ordering","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Ordering depends on Payments for charge results; Payments defines the payment API contract but accommodates Ordering's needs (idempotency, status reporting)."},{"upstream":"Ordering","downstream":"Notifications","relationship":"Customer-Supplier via Outbox — Ordering publishes domain events (OrderConfirmed) through a transactional outbox; Notifications consumes them. Notifications is a downstream conformist to the event schema."},{"upstream":"Stripe Gateway","downstream":"Payments","relationship":"Conformist with Anti-Corruption Layer — Payments conforms to Stripe's API but wraps it in an ACL to translate Stripe-specific error codes, webhook formats, and status semantics into its own domain language."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Keep Order→Payment synchronous (with resilience patterns) rather than go fully async","gains":"Simpler user experience — customer gets immediate payment confirmation. Easier to reason about order state. No need for polling or websockets on the frontend.","sacrifices":"Temporal coupling remains. Order Service availability is bounded by Payment Service availability (mitigated but not eliminated by circuit breaker). Latency floor is sum of both services.","alternatives_considered":["Fully async payment processing with status polling — eliminates temporal coupling but adds UX complexity and eventual consistency concerns","Choreography-based saga with events — more decoupled but harder to trace and debug payment flows"]},{"decision":"Transactional Outbox for notifications instead of direct async HTTP","gains":"At-least-once delivery guarantee. Notification intent is captured atomically with order state change. Survives Notification Service outages. Auditable.","sacrifices":"Added infrastructure complexity (outbox table, relay/polling process or CDC). Slight latency increase for email delivery. Need to handle duplicate delivery in Notification Service (idempotent consumers).","alternatives_considered":["Message broker (RabbitMQ/Kafka) — stronger guarantees but adds infrastructure dependency and operational burden for what may be a small-scale system","Keep fire-and-forget but add reconciliation job — simpler but reconciliation has latency and may miss edge cases"]},{"decision":"Circuit Breaker at Order→Payment boundary","gains":"Fail-fast behavior during outages. Prevents thread pool exhaustion. Gives Payment Service time to recover without being hammered.","sacrifices":"During open-circuit state, ALL orders fail — even if some might succeed. Need fallback UX (e.g., 'try again later' or queue for retry). Tuning thresholds requires production traffic data.","alternatives_considered":["Only retries without circuit breaker — risks overwhelming a struggling Payment Service","Async queue between Order and Payment — eliminates need for circuit breaker but changes the user experience model entirely"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Payment Gateway","evolution_path":"The ACL in Payment Service means swapping Stripe for another gateway (Adyen, Braintree) requires changes only inside Payment Service. No changes to Order Service or its contract."},{"area":"Notification Channels","evolution_path":"Outbox events carry notification intent, not channel specifics. Notification Service can evolve to add SMS, push notifications, or webhooks without changing the Ordering context."},{"area":"Outbox Relay Mechanism","evolution_path":"Start with polling-based outbox relay. Evolve to CDC (Debezium) or replace with a message broker when scale demands it. The outbox table is the stable contract; the relay is swappable."},{"area":"Payment Retry Strategy","evolution_path":"Retry policies (count, backoff, jitter) should be externalized to configuration. Can be tuned per-environment without code changes."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Synchronous Order→Payment→Stripe call chain","reason":"This is an intentional rigidity. The business requires immediate payment confirmation for order acceptance. Changing this to async fundamentally alters the product UX and requires frontend changes, state machine redesign, and customer communication changes. Protect this decision unless business requirements change."},{"area":"Idempotency key generation","reason":"The contract between Order Service and Payment Service MUST include idempotency keys from day one. Retrofitting idempotency into an existing system with production data and in-flight transactions is extremely dangerous. This must be rigid and correct from the start."},{"area":"Order state machine transitions","reason":"Order states (created→payment_pending→paid→confirmed→failed) and their allowed transitions are critical invariants. Incorrect transitions lead to double-charges, lost orders, or phantom confirmations. This must be enforced at the aggregate level with no backdoors."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Double-charging customers due to retry without idempotency","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Every payment request MUST carry an idempotency key generated by Order Service (e.g., orderId + attempt number). Payment Service forwards this to Stripe. Stripe natively supports idempotency keys. Payment Service must also check its own records before calling Stripe to short-circuit known-completed charges."},{"risk":"Cascading failure: Stripe outage → Payment Service timeout → Order Service thread pool exhaustion → entire Order Service unresponsive","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Three-layer defense: (1) Timeout budget — Payment Service gives Stripe 5s max, Order Service gives Payment Service 8s max. (2) Bulkhead — dedicated connection pool for payment calls, sized to prevent total exhaustion. (3) Circuit Breaker — after N consecutive failures in a window, stop calling Payment Service entirely and return a clear 'payment unavailable' response."},{"risk":"Silent notification loss — fire-and-forget drops emails permanently","severity":"high","mitigation":"Replace fire-and-forget with transactional outbox. Order Service writes a notification_outbox row in the same DB transaction that updates order status to 'confirmed'. A relay process delivers to Notification Service with retries. Failed deliveries go to a DLQ. Add monitoring/alerting on outbox lag and DLQ depth."},{"risk":"Payment succeeds at Stripe but Order Service fails to persist the result (crash, DB error after receiving payment response)","severity":"high","mitigation":"Compensation pattern: (1) Payment Service records successful charge in its own DB before returning response. (2) A reconciliation job compares Payment records vs Order records periodically. (3) Unmatched successful payments trigger automatic refund or manual review. (4) Stripe webhooks provide a secondary confirmation channel — Payment Service listens for charge.succeeded events as a safety net."},{"risk":"Timeout ambiguity — Stripe call times out but charge actually succeeded","severity":"high","mitigation":"This is the classic 'unknown' state. Payment Service must treat timeouts as 'unknown', NOT as failures. On retry, the idempotency key ensures Stripe returns the original result. Payment Service should also query Stripe (GET /charges) before retrying to resolve the ambiguous state. Order should remain in 'payment_pending' until resolved, never auto-fail on timeout."},{"risk":"Notification Service outage causes outbox table to grow unbounded","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Set retention policy on outbox (e.g., 7 days). Alert on outbox depth > threshold. The relay should have configurable batch sizes and backoff. Stale entries beyond retention move to cold storage or DLQ for investigation."},{"risk":"Circuit breaker oscillation during partial Stripe degradation","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Use a half-open state with gradual traffic ramping (e.g., allow 10% of requests through during half-open). Tune failure thresholds based on observed error rates, not just consecutive failures. Consider using a sliding window rate-based breaker rather than count-based."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Phase 1 (immediate, blocks launch): Add idempotency keys to Payment API contract, add timeouts to all HTTP calls, implement basic retry with backoff on Payment→Stripe. Phase 2 (before production traffic): Add circuit breaker (Order→Payment), bulkhead isolation, replace fire-and-forget notifications with transactional outbox. Phase 3 (post-launch hardening): Add Stripe webhook listener as secondary confirmation channel, build reconciliation job, add DLQ processing dashboard, tune circuit breaker thresholds from production metrics.","files_to_create_or_modify":["order-service/src/payment-client.ts — Add timeout, retry, circuit breaker, idempotency key propagation","order-service/src/outbox/notification-outbox.ts — Transactional outbox table writes","order-service/src/outbox/outbox-relay.ts — Polling relay that reads outbox and delivers to Notification Service","order-service/db/migrations/add_notification_outbox.sql — Outbox table schema (id, event_type, payload, created_at, processed_at, status)","payment-service/src/stripe-acl.ts — Anti-corruption layer translating Stripe responses/errors into domain language","payment-service/src/idempotency/idempotency-store.ts — Record of idempotency keys and their outcomes for short-circuit logic","payment-service/src/handlers/charge-handler.ts — Add idempotency check before Stripe call, handle timeout-as-unknown","payment-service/db/migrations/add_payment_attempts.sql — Payment attempt records with idempotency keys","notification-service/src/handlers/notification-handler.ts — Make handler idempotent (check notificationId before processing)"],"patterns_to_follow":"Use well-tested libraries for resilience primitives (e.g., opossum for circuit breakers in Node.js, resilience4j for JVM). Do NOT hand-roll circuit breaker state machines. Outbox relay should use SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED for safe concurrent processing. All HTTP clients must have explicit connect and read timeouts — never rely on defaults. Idempotency keys should be UUIDs or deterministic hashes (orderId + operation), never sequential.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["NEVER treat a payment timeout as a failure — it is an unknown state that must be resolved before proceeding","NEVER retry a payment without an idempotency key — this is how double-charges happen","Do not put business logic in the circuit breaker fallback — it should return a clear error, not try to 'work around' missing payments","Do not use in-memory outbox — it must be in the same database and same transaction as the order state change, or it provides zero guarantees","Avoid retry storms — always use jitter in backoff to decorrelate retries across concurrent requests","Do not set circuit breaker thresholds too aggressively — a single transient failure should not open the circuit; use a rate-based window"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":92,"high_confidence_areas":["Failure mode enumeration — these are well-understood distributed systems failure modes","Idempotency requirement — this is non-negotiable for any payment retry path","Outbox pattern for notifications — the fire-and-forget gap is clear and the fix is well-established","Timeout and circuit breaker necessity — standard resilience patterns for synchronous chains"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact timeout values and circuit breaker thresholds — these require production traffic data to tune properly","Whether the outbox relay should use polling vs CDC — depends on scale and existing infrastructure, which are unknown","Whether synchronous payment is the right business choice — assumed yes based on the described architecture, but async payment with a 'processing' state might be acceptable to the business"],"assumptions":["The business requires immediate payment confirmation (justifying synchronous chain)","Each service owns its own database (no shared DB)","Stripe idempotency key support is available and will be used","Order volumes are moderate enough that a polling-based outbox relay is sufficient initially","The system uses a relational database that supports transactions (for outbox pattern)"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"This synchronous-chain architecture (Order→Payment→Stripe) creates a cascading failure corridor where any downstream outage propagates upstream as customer-facing errors. The fire-and-forget notification path silently drops emails with no recovery. The design needs circuit breakers, retries with backoff, an outbox pattern for notifications, and a saga/compensation mechanism for partial failures across the Order-Payment boundary.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Order
